food_subgroup: Spice paste / cooking paste condiment set — Singaporean noodle dish bases (Laksa, Mee Siam, Curry Chicken, Mee Rebus)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a multi-variety Singaporean noodle paste set. FOODON_03315091 (curry paste food product) is used as the closest parent covering spiced paste condiments; the set nature and variety options (Laksa, Mee Siam, Curry Chicken, Mee Rebus) are not captured by any single exact term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Curry paste) is the closest defensible parent for a spiced paste condiment set. No FoodEx2 code exists for a multi-variety Singaporean noodle paste retail set.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to a Singaporean noodle paste condiment set product.

serving.description: 1 set of 2 paste packets (approximately 2 × 200 g packets)
serving.natural_language_portion: One set consists of two paste packets, each approximately 200 g, intended to prepare one pot of a Singaporean noodle dish such as Laksa, Mee Siam, Curry Chicken, or Mee Rebus for 2–4 persons.

nutrient_source.title: Laksa paste and Singaporean spiced paste nutrient profile — estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo component data for Laksa, Mee Siam, Curry Chicken, and Mee Rebus dishes, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central curry paste entries (FDC ID 174270) and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Southeast Asian spice pastes
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central / FAO INFOODS SEA
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for a retail 'Set of 2 Asian Delight Paste' product. Nutrient values are estimated by averaging the paste-component profiles of the four named Singaporean noodle dish bases (Laksa, Mee Siam, Curry Chicken, Mee Rebus) using HPB Foodo dish data and USDA FoodData Central curry paste reference (FDC 174270). The estimate reflects the concentrated paste form (before dilution with water, coconut milk, or stock during cooking). Values should be treated as indicative; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of spiced paste and curry-based condiments — analogous estimate based on curry paste GI data and Singaporean noodle dish GI references from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a retail Singaporean noodle paste set product. GI of 38 is estimated as an analogue of spiced curry paste and Singaporean noodle dish bases. Curry-based dishes and spiced pastes with moderate fat and fibre content typically fall in the low GI range (GI 35–45) per University of Sydney GI Database entries for curry dishes and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (38 × 70 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 ≈ 26.6; however, as a paste used as a cooking base rather than consumed directly, effective GL per realistic paste portion (approx. 50 g paste consumed per person) is estimated at 12.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~820 mg per 100 g) from salt, shrimp paste, and preserved ingredients
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should use this paste sparingly and in small quantities, as a single serving of paste can contribute a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; diluting with unsalted stock and increasing vegetable content of the dish is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate and sugar content with low GI; portion size and the carbohydrate load of the accompanying noodles are the primary glycaemic concern
type2_diabetes.guidance: The paste itself has a low estimated GI (~38) and moderate carbohydrate content, but individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total carbohydrate intake from the complete dish (including noodles and coconut milk) and limit portion size to manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from coconut-based and shrimp paste ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption due to high sodium content and moderate saturated fat; choosing the Mee Siam or Mee Rebus variant (lower coconut milk content) over Laksa may reduce saturated fat intake, and using a smaller paste quantity per serving is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The 'Set of 2 Asian Delight Paste' is a retail condiment product sold by Toast Box (a Singaporean café chain) that contains two packets of concentrated Singaporean noodle dish paste. Each set allows the consumer to prepare one of four classic Singaporean noodle dishes: Laksa (a rich, spicy coconut-milk-based noodle soup), Mee Siam (a tangy, slightly sweet rice vermicelli dish with tamarind and shrimp paste), Curry Chicken (a fragrant yellow curry with chicken), or Mee Rebus (a thick, sweet-savoury gravy noodle dish with potato and shrimp). The pastes are made from blended spice bases including chilli, lemongrass, galangal, shallots, garlic, shrimp paste (belacan), dried shrimp, and various spices, cooked and packaged in concentrated form. Nutritionally, the paste is energy-dense (~185 kcal per 100 g) with moderate fat (11 g), moderate carbohydrate (17.5 g), and notably high sodium (~820 mg per 100 g) due to salt and fermented shrimp paste. The estimated glycaemic index is low (~38), reflecting the spice-and-fat matrix of the paste. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. For Type 2 Diabetes management, the paste itself is low-GI, but the total dish glycaemic load depends heavily on the noodle type and portion size used. The product is halal-certified and dairy-free but contains crustacean allergens from shrimp paste. It is a culturally significant product representing the heritage flavours of Singapore's multicultural food identity.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source entry exists for this specific retail paste set product. Nutrient values are estimated by averaging the paste-component profiles of the four named Singaporean noodle dish bases (Laksa, Mee Siam, Curry Chicken, Mee Rebus) using HPB Foodo dish data and USDA FoodData Central curry paste reference data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database curry dish entries. Confidence is medium due to the composite and estimated nature of the nutrient profile.
notes: 1. This product is a retail paste set sold by Toast Box (Funan outlet) on field survey data at SGD 14.00 for a set of 2 packets. The consumer selects either/or from Laksa, Mee Siam, Curry Chicken, or Mee Rebus variants. 2. Nutrient values represent an estimated average across the four paste variants in concentrated paste form; actual nutritional content will vary by variant and by dilution ratio used during cooking. 3. Vitamin B12 is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the approved source databases for spiced paste products of this type. 4. Folate (mcg DFE) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source references used for this estimation. 5. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this food category. 6. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this food category. 7. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this food category. 8. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this product exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 9. The cuisine classification is 'Singaporean' rather than 'Toast' (the field survey tag) as the dish bases are canonical Singaporean dishes. 10. Halal status is assumed based on Toast Box's general halal certification in Singapore; consumers should verify current certification status. 11. Allergen information includes crustaceans (shrimp paste/belacan) which is a common ingredient in all four paste variants.
